chat.u: Today my manager asked me why I chose a particular approach in the task and I froze for a second even though I had a reason. After that I kept replaying it in my head like maybe I sounded confused and now he thinks I do not know what I am doing.
It is only been a little while since I joined but I already feel like everyone else speaks with so much confidence. I am scared that I am just good at pretending I understand things.
A senior asked me to explain my update in the standup and I made it longer than needed because I was nervous. Then later I felt embarrassed because concise answers seem so natural for others.
Nobody said anything bad directly, but I could feel myself searching their faces for disappointment. It is tiring to turn every normal work conversation into proof that I am not competent.
I wish I could just learn without constantly judging myself for not already knowing everything. This job is supposed to be the start, but my mind treats it like a final exam every day.
chat.r: Noted. Say more if you'd like.

chat.u: I got feedback on my pull request today and most of it was reasonable, but one comment said I missed an edge case. That one line stayed with me the whole evening.
I know reviews are normal and everyone gets them, but when it happens to me I feel exposed. Like they have finally found the gap I was trying to hide.
I picked up my guitar for 20 minutes today after a long time. It felt nice to do something where I am allowed to be bad and still enjoy learning.
Music makes me feel like there is a part of me outside work performance. I want to protect that somehow instead of letting office stress eat every evening.
Someone from another team asked why my task was taking longer than expected and I felt defensive immediately. I answered politely, but inside I was panicking that now people think I am slow.
The hard part is I am genuinely still learning the codebase, so sometimes things do take longer. But instead of seeing that as normal, I turn it into a character flaw.
I stayed late trying to make up for it, and now I am exhausted. I am not sure if I am working hard or just trying to outrun the feeling that I do not belong.
My manager said the output was okay but asked me to be clearer about tradeoffs next time. I heard the words normally, but my brain translated it into you are not thinking deeply enough.
I hate how quickly feedback becomes identity for me. It is not just that the work needs improvement, it becomes I need improvement as a person.
Today was better because I got through a task without anyone questioning me much. But even then I kept expecting some hidden mistake to appear later.
It feels like my confidence depends too much on external validation. If no one criticizes me, I am okay for a while, but one question can undo the whole day.
I listened to some old songs I used to practice in college and remembered how much I liked figuring out melodies by ear. I miss having that kind of curiosity without pressure.
Maybe I should keep one evening a week for music properly. Not as another productivity goal, just as something that keeps me connected to myself.
In a meeting today someone challenged my estimate and asked if I had considered dependencies. I had considered some, but not all, and I felt my face get hot immediately.
After the call I kept thinking maybe this is why they should not have hired me. That sounds dramatic, but the thought comes so fast when I am questioned in front of others.
I do not mind learning from mistakes in theory. I just hate the public moment where I realize I missed something and everyone else realizes it too.

My manager actually told me today that asking for clarification earlier would help, and he said it pretty calmly. I think I wait too long because I am scared questions will reveal I am not good enough.
The irony is that by not asking, I make the work harder and then feel even more incompetent. It is like I am protecting an image that is already stressing me out.
A teammate reviewed my change and said the implementation was clean, and I felt relieved for maybe ten minutes. Then I started thinking maybe it was an easy task and that is why it went well.
It is annoying that even positive feedback does not fully land. My mind always finds a way to discount it before it can help.
I practiced guitar again tonight and tried a song I used to avoid because the chord changes were hard. It reminded me that progress can be slow and still real.
I wish I could apply that same patience to work. With music I can repeat a mistake without calling myself a failure, but with office work I become harsh immediately.
Today someone asked me if I was sure about a small decision I made, and even that simple question triggered me. I started explaining too much because I wanted to prove I had thought about it.
Later I realized they may have just been checking context, not doubting me. But in the moment every question sounds like an accusation.
I want to learn the difference between being challenged and being exposed. Right now both feel the same in my body.
I had a one-on-one today and my manager said I am ramping up fine but need to communicate blockers earlier. That was probably a fair point, but I still came home feeling like I had been marked down.
Maybe I keep expecting feedback to be either praise or proof that I am failing. I do not know how to hold it as just information yet.
I noticed today that I did ask one question earlier instead of hiding confusion. It felt uncomfortable, but the answer saved me from going in the wrong direction for hours.
Maybe asking is not as embarrassing as I make it in my head. It still feels risky, but at least today it helped.
I spent some time learning a new song tutorial today and did not rush through it. I liked having a learning curve that was mine and not being judged by a sprint board or review comments.
It sounds small, but music makes the week feel less like one long test of competence. I want to keep that space alive.
There was another review today with a few suggestions, and I still felt that sting at first. But I did not spiral as badly as earlier this month.
I tried to separate the comment from my whole identity and just wrote down what needed to change. It felt mechanical, but it helped.
I am not suddenly confident, but I can see that questions at work do not always mean I am failing. Sometimes they are just part of how work gets better.
Looking back at this month, I still get triggered when someone questions my work, especially in public. But I also handled a few moments better than I expected.
I think the imposter feeling is still there, but it is not the only story now. There is also evidence that I am learning, asking better questions, and slowly finding space for music again.
